section.title: The Waters Recede
section.sectionRationale: The turning point of the flood. God remembers Noah, the waters go down, the ark rests, and a new world dries out. Noah steps onto cleansed ground and builds an altar, and God answers with a heartfelt promise of stability for the earth. The chapter is one arc, from the wind that dries the waters to the pledge that seedtime and harvest will never cease.
section.thesis: God remembers His own, brings them safely onto a new earth, and, moved by the pleasing aroma of sacrifice, pledges enduring mercy to a world whose heart is still bent toward evil.
section.frameNote: Four movements: God remembers and the waters subside; the raven and the dove; coming out onto a new earth; and the altar and God's promise of stability.

verses.text: But God {{remembered|remembered}} Noah and all the animals and livestock that were with him in the ark. And God sent a wind over the earth, and the waters began to subside.
The springs of the deep and the floodgates of the heavens were closed, and the rain from the sky was restrained.
The waters receded steadily from the earth, and after 150 days the waters had gone down.
On the seventeenth day of the seventh month, the ark came to {{rest|rest}} on the mountains of Ararat.
And the waters continued to recede until the tenth month, and on the first day of the tenth month the tops of the mountains became visible.
After forty days Noah opened the window he had made in the ark
and sent out a raven. It kept flying back and forth until the waters had dried up from the earth.
Then Noah sent out a dove to see if the waters had receded from the surface of the ground.

But the dove found no place to rest her foot, and she returned to him in the ark, because the waters were still covering the surface of all the earth. So he reached out his hand and brought her back inside the ark.
Noah waited seven more days and again sent out the dove from the ark.
And behold, the dove returned to him in the evening with a freshly plucked olive leaf in her beak. So Noah knew that the waters had receded from the earth.
And Noah waited seven more days and sent out the dove again, but this time she did not return to him.

units.label: Coming out onto a new earth

verses.text: In Noah’s six hundred and first year, on the first day of the first month, the waters had dried up from the earth. So Noah removed the covering from the ark and saw that the surface of the ground was dry.
By the twenty-seventh day of the second month, the earth was fully dry.
Then God said to Noah,
“Come out of the ark, you and your wife, along with your sons and their wives.
Bring out all the living creatures that are with you, birds, livestock, and everything that crawls upon the ground, so that they can spread out over the earth and be fruitful and multiply upon it.”
So Noah came out, along with his sons and his wife and his sons’ wives.
Every living creature, every creeping thing, and every bird, everything that moves upon the earth, came out of the ark, kind by kind.

units.label: The altar and God's promise
units.note: a pleasing aroma, an enduring mercy

verses.text: Then Noah built an {{altar|altar}} to the LORD. And taking from every kind of clean animal and clean bird, he offered burnt offerings on the altar.

When the LORD smelled the pleasing {{aroma|aroma}}, He said in His heart, “Never again will I curse the ground because of man, even though every {{inclination|inclination}} of his heart is evil from his youth. And never again will I destroy all living creatures as I have done.
As long as the earth endures, seedtime and harvest, cold and heat, summer and winter, day and night shall never cease.”

remembered.gloss: To remember, to call to mind and act. When God remembers, it is never that He had forgotten; it means He moves to fulfill His commitment. God remembered Noah, and at once the waters began to subside. The word marks the turning point of the whole flood, the hinge on which judgment gives way to salvation.

rest.gloss: To rest, to settle down. The ark came to rest on the mountains of Ararat. The verb is the root behind Noah's own name and the comfort his father hoped for; after the storm of judgment, the vessel of salvation finds rest, a small foretaste of the rest God gives His people.

altar.gloss: An altar, literally a place of slaughter or sacrifice. This is the first altar named in the Bible. Noah's first act on the new earth is worship, offering clean animals to the God who saved him; the renewed world begins, as creation did, oriented toward the worship of God.

aroma.gloss: A soothing or pleasing aroma. The LORD smelled the pleasing aroma of Noah's offering and spoke words of mercy. The phrase recurs throughout the sacrificial law and finally describes Christ's self-offering, a fragrant sacrifice to God, Ephesians 5:2; acceptable sacrifice moves God to bless.

inclination.gloss: The bent or framing of the heart. In 6:5 the yetser of man's heart was the reason for judgment; here, remarkably, the very same evil inclination from youth becomes the occasion for mercy. God's promise not to curse the ground again rests not on human improvement but on His own grace.

basic.context: The flood is ending. God remembers Noah, and the waters go down until the ark rests on a mountain. Noah tests the land with a raven and a dove, and at last the dove brings back an olive leaf. When the earth is dry, Noah comes out and builds an altar to worship God. God then promises never again to destroy the world by flood.
basic.hermeneutics: This really happened, and it marks the turn from judgment to new beginning. A key question is why God promises never to flood the earth again, since people are still sinful. The answer is grace: God is moved by Noah's sacrifice and, out of mercy, pledges stability to the world despite the human heart.
basic.originalLanguages: God remembered, zakar, Noah, which means He acted on His promise. The ark came to rest, nuach, the root of Noah's own name. Noah builds the first altar, mizbeach, in the Bible, and the LORD smells the pleasing aroma, reach nichoach. Strikingly, the same evil inclination, yetser, that caused the flood is now the backdrop for God's mercy.
basic.typology: Noah's altar and pleasing sacrifice point to Christ, whose offering is a truly pleasing aroma to God. The ark coming to rest and the new, cleansed earth foreshadow the rest and new creation Christ secures. The dove with the olive leaf becomes a lasting picture of peace after judgment.
basic.christ: The sacrifice that pleases God and brings words of mercy points to Jesus, whose self-offering is the fragrant sacrifice that truly turns away wrath. And God's promise of stability rests on grace, the same grace fully revealed at the cross, where mercy meets a world that does not deserve it.
basic.god: God remembers His own and acts to save. He is moved by worship and responds with mercy, promising to uphold the rhythms of the world, seedtime and harvest, day and night. He commits Himself to patient kindness toward a world whose heart is still bent toward evil.
basic.crossReferences: Ephesians 5 calls Christ's offering a fragrant sacrifice to God. Hebrews 4 speaks of the rest God gives His people. 2 Peter 3 recalls God's patience. Acts 14 points to seedtime and harvest as God's ongoing kindness.
basic.oneStory: Judgment gives way to grace. God remembers Noah, brings him onto a cleansed earth, and, moved by sacrifice, pledges enduring mercy to a still sinful world. This new beginning and this pleasing offering press forward toward Christ, the true sacrifice, and toward the final rest and new creation He brings.

context.literary: The structural center of the flood narrative, pivoting on God remembered Noah. The account mirrors creation in reverse-then-forward: waters recede, dry land appears, birds and creatures come out, and the section climaxes in worship and a divine promise given in poetic form.
context.historical: The precise dates and the resting on the mountains of Ararat present the account as history. Noah's altar and burnt offerings reflect the ancient pattern of sacrifice, and God's promise secured the agricultural rhythms on which Israel's life depended.
context.canonical: The flood's end launches a renewed creation mandate, be fruitful and multiply, echoing Genesis 1, and the pleasing aroma of sacrifice opens a theme fulfilled in Christ. God's pledge of stability underlies the common grace the New Testament affirms.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To narrate God's faithful remembrance of Noah, the emergence of a cleansed earth, Noah's worship, and God's gracious promise to sustain the world despite ongoing human sin.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The account describes God's actions and promise; Noah's worship models the fitting response of gratitude. God's pledge in verses 21 to 22 is a standing promise, not merely a past event.

debates.question: On what basis does God promise never again to destroy the earth by flood?

views.label: The accepted sacrifice moves God to mercy
views.case: The promise follows immediately upon the LORD smelling the pleasing aroma of Noah's offering; the text ties God's gracious words to acceptable, atoning worship, foreshadowing the sacrifice of Christ.

views.label: God's sheer grace despite unchanged human nature
views.case: God grounds the promise in the sobering fact that the human heart is evil from youth; humanity has not improved, so the pledge rests entirely on God's mercy and patience, not on any human merit.

debates.bearing: The two meet in the gospel: acceptable sacrifice and free grace are not rivals, for the sacrifice that pleases God is itself His gracious provision, fulfilled in Christ, on the basis of which God shows patient mercy to sinners.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter turns on a single verb: God zakar, remembered, Noah, not recalling something forgotten but moving to keep His commitment, and instantly the waters begin to fall. The ark then nuach, comes to rest, the very root of Noah's name and the comfort his father longed for; after the storm, salvation settles on solid ground. On the new earth Noah builds the first mizbeach, altar, in Scripture, and the LORD smells the reach nichoach, the pleasing aroma, the phrase that will echo through the sacrificial law and land at last on Christ's own fragrant offering. Most striking of all, the yetser, the evil inclination of the heart named as the cause of judgment in 6:5, reappears here, unchanged, as the very backdrop against which God speaks mercy. Grace, not human improvement, is the ground of the promise.

typology.fulfillment: Christ's self-offering, a fragrant sacrifice to God that truly turns away wrath, Ephesians 5:2.

typology.type: The ark coming to rest on a cleansed earth
typology.fulfillment: The rest and new creation secured by Christ for His people, Hebrews 4:9-10, Revelation 21:1-5.

typology.type: The dove returning with the olive leaf
typology.fulfillment: A lasting sign of peace after judgment, anticipating the peace God makes with the world through Christ.

study.christ: The climax of the chapter preaches the gospel. Noah's burnt offering rises as a pleasing aroma, and God responds with words of mercy toward a world that has not earned them; the New Testament takes up that very phrase to describe Christ, who gave Himself up for us as a fragrant offering and sacrifice to God, Ephesians 5:2. It is the accepted sacrifice, ultimately Christ's, that grounds God's patient mercy toward sinners whose hearts remain bent toward evil. The ark coming to rest on a cleansed earth foreshadows the rest Christ gives and the new creation He will bring, while the dove and olive leaf remain an enduring emblem of the peace God makes through the cross.
study.god: God is faithful, gracious, and patient. He remembers His own and acts to save at the exact moment of need. He is a God who receives worship and is moved by it, responding to Noah's sacrifice with a tender resolve of mercy. Most remarkable is the ground of His promise: knowing full well that the human heart is evil from youth, He nonetheless pledges to uphold the world and never again to destroy all life by flood. His commitment to the seasons and rhythms of creation, seedtime and harvest, cold and heat, is an act of steady kindness to a world that continues to sin, a preview of the patience by which He waits and saves.

crossReferences.note: Christ loved us and gave Himself up for us as a fragrant offering and sacrifice to God, fulfilling the pleasing aroma of Noah's altar.
There remains a Sabbath rest for the people of God, the true rest anticipated by the ark's resting.
The Lord is patient, not wanting anyone to perish, the same patient mercy shown after the flood.
God gives rains and fruitful seasons, the ongoing kindness pledged in the promise of seedtime and harvest.

study.oneStory: At the flood's turning point God remembers Noah, and judgment gives way to a cleansed, new earth. Noah's first act is worship, and the pleasing aroma of his sacrifice draws from God a promise of enduring mercy to a still sinful world. This new beginning, this fragrant offering, and this pledge of patient grace all press forward toward Christ, whose sacrifice truly pleases God, and toward the final rest and new creation He secures.

quiz.q: What does it mean that God remembered Noah?

options.text: God moved to act on His commitment to save, and the waters began to subside.
options.note: God's remembering is His faithful action, not the recovery of a forgotten thing.

options.text: God had forgotten Noah and suddenly recalled him.
options.note: God never forgot; remembering here means He acted to fulfill His promise.

options.text: Noah reminded God to help by praying hard enough.
options.note: The initiative is God's; the text says God remembered Noah, not the reverse.

options.text: It means nothing in particular.
options.note: It is the hinge of the whole account, turning judgment toward salvation.

quiz.q: What is the first thing Noah does when he comes out onto the new earth?

options.text: He builds an altar and offers worship to the LORD who saved him.
options.note: The renewed world begins with worship at the first altar in the Bible.

options.text: He plants crops to secure the next harvest.
options.note: That comes later; his first act is building an altar to worship.

options.text: He sets out to explore the mountains.
options.note: The text highlights worship, not exploration, as his first act.

options.text: He complains about all he has lost.
options.note: Noah responds with worship and thanksgiving, not complaint.

quiz.q: On what basis does God promise never again to destroy the earth by flood?

options.text: On His own grace, moved by the pleasing sacrifice, even though the human heart is still evil from youth.
options.note: The promise rests on grace and accepted sacrifice, not on human improvement.

options.text: Because humanity had finally become good after the flood.
options.note: The opposite; God notes the heart is evil from youth, yet still promises mercy.

options.text: Because there was no more water left anywhere.
options.note: The promise is a moral pledge of mercy, not a comment on water supply.

options.text: Because Noah bargained a deal with God.
options.note: There is no bargain; God freely pledges mercy after the pleasing offering.

quiz.q: How does Noah's pleasing sacrifice point to Christ?

options.text: Its pleasing aroma foreshadows Christ's self-offering, called a fragrant sacrifice to God.
options.note: Ephesians 5:2 uses the same imagery for Christ's sacrifice.

options.text: It shows that God is pleased only by expensive gifts.
options.note: The point is acceptable, atoning sacrifice, not the cost of the gift.

options.text: It teaches that we must offer animals today.
options.note: The sacrifices point forward to Christ, who fulfills them once for all.

options.text: It has no link to Christ.
options.note: The New Testament ties the pleasing aroma directly to Christ's offering.

quiz.q: What does the ark coming to rest signify?

options.text: After the storm of judgment, salvation settles safely, a foretaste of the rest God gives.
options.note: The word for rest is the root of Noah's name and points to God-given rest.

options.text: That the ark was finally falling apart.
options.note: Resting means settling securely, not breaking down.

options.text: That Noah's journey had failed.
options.note: The resting marks success and safety, the turn toward a new world.

options.text: Nothing beyond a change of location.
options.note: The resting carries the deeper note of God-given rest after judgment.

questions: God remembered Noah at exactly the right moment. Where are you waiting on God right now, and how does it help to know that His remembering is His acting, even when the waters have not yet gone down?
Noah's first act on the new earth was to worship. When God brings you through a hard season, is your first instinct gratitude and worship, or something else?
God promised mercy knowing full well that the human heart is still bent toward evil. How does it change you to realize God's kindness rests on His grace and not on your improvement?
The dove returned with an olive leaf, a small sign that new life was possible again. Where do you need to watch for the small signs of God's mercy breaking through after a flood in your own life?
God pledged to keep seedtime and harvest, day and night, faithfully turning. How might noticing God's steady, everyday kindness in ordinary rhythms deepen your trust in Him this week?

translationNotes.note: God remembered Noah does not imply God had forgotten; the Hebrew idiom for remembering describes God acting to fulfill a commitment, and it marks the decisive turn from judgment to salvation.

The pleasing aroma renders a phrase, a soothing aroma, used repeatedly in the sacrificial law; the same imagery is applied to Christ's offering in Ephesians 5:2, so acceptable sacrifice is bound up with God's words of mercy here.
